column1: What was the name of the international body formed in the 1870s to coordinate socialist efforts across Europe?
column2: The Second International

column1: Which German political party worked closely with workers' associations to win parliamentary seats by 1905?
column2: The Social Democratic Party (SPD)

column1: In which two countries were the Labour Party and the Socialist Party formed by 1905?
column2: Britain and France

column1: Which monk became a symbol of the unpopularity of the Russian autocracy due to his influence over Tsarina Alexandra?
column2: Rasputin

column1: On which day did the February Revolution begin with a lockout at a factory and sympathetic strikes in Petrograd?
column2: 22 February 1917

column1: What was the name of the peace treaty signed between the Bolsheviks and Germany in March 1918?
column2: The Treaty of Brest Litovsk

column1: What group in 19th-century Europe wanted a nation that tolerated all religions and protected individual rights against the government?
column2: Liberals

column1: What was the liberal stance on universal adult franchise?
column2: They did not believe in it, arguing instead that only men of property should have the right to vote.

column1: Which political group wanted a government based on the majority of a country's population and supported the women's suffragette movement?
column2: Radicals

column1: How did radicals differ from liberals regarding the privileges of the wealthy?
column2: Radicals opposed the privileges of great landowners and wealthy factory owners, whereas liberals often were property owners themselves.

column1: What was the core belief of conservatives regarding the process of societal change in the 19th century?
column2: They believed change was inevitable but should be brought about through a slow process that respected the past.

column1: Term: Suffragette movement
column2: Definition: A political movement aimed at giving women the right to vote.

column1: What were the typical working conditions brought about by early industrialization in Europe?
column2: Workers faced long hours, poor wages, and frequent unemployment during periods of low demand.

column1: Why did liberals and radicals often search for solutions to housing and sanitation problems in growing towns?
column2: They were often property owners and employers who believed a healthy and educated workforce would benefit the economy.

column1: Which Italian nationalist conspired after 1815 to create a nation where all citizens had equal rights?
column2: Giuseppe Mazzini

column1: What did socialists consider to be the root of all social ills in the mid-19th century?
column2: Private property

column1: What cooperative community did Robert Owen seek to build in Indiana, USA?
column2: New Harmony

column1: How did Louis Blanc's vision for cooperatives differ from Robert Owen's?
column2: Blanc demanded that the government encourage and replace capitalist enterprises with cooperatives, rather than relying solely on individual initiative.

column1: According to Karl Marx, who produced the profit accumulated by capitalists in an industrial society?
column2: The workers

column1: What did Karl Marx believe was the only way for workers to free themselves from capitalist exploitation?
column2: They had to construct a radically socialist society where all property was socially controlled.

column1: Who was the ruler of the Russian Empire in 1914?
column2: Tsar Nicholas II

column1: What was the majority religion in the Russian Empire at the start of the 20th century?
column2: Russian Orthodox Christianity

column1: What percentage of the Russian Empire's population earned their living from agriculture in the early 20th century?
column2: About 85 per cent

column1: Name the two most prominent industrial areas in Russia during the late 19th and early 20th centuries.
column2: St Petersburg and Moscow

column1: Why did coal, iron, and steel production increase rapidly in Russia during the 1890s?
column2: The railway network was extended and foreign investment in industry increased.

column1: Which group of workers in St Petersburg considered themselves the "aristocrats" of the labor force due to their high skill level?
column2: Metalworkers

column1: What was the status of women in the Russian factory labor force by 1914 regarding their wages compared to men?
column2: They made up 31 per cent of the workforce but were paid between half and three-quarters of a man's wage.

column1: Term: Mir
column2: Definition: The Russian peasant commune that periodically pooled and redistributed land according to family needs.

column1: How did Russian peasants differ from French peasants in their attitude toward the nobility?
column2: Russian peasants had little respect for the nobility and often refused to pay rent or murdered landlords to get their land.

column1: Which political party was founded in 1898 by Russian socialists who respected Karl Marx's ideas?
column2: The Russian Social Democratic Workers Party

column1: What was the primary demand of the Socialist Revolutionary Party formed in 1900?
column2: They demanded that land belonging to nobles be transferred to peasants.

column1: Why did Vladimir Lenin disagree with the Socialist Revolutionaries' view of peasants as a united revolutionary force?
column2: He argued that peasants were divided into poor laborers and wealthy capitalists, making them an inconsistent group for a socialist movement.

column1: What were the two factions of the Russian Social Democratic Workers Party, and how did they differ in their view of party membership?
column2: The Bolsheviks wanted a disciplined, restricted membership, while the Mensheviks believed the party should be open to all.

column1: Who were the "jadidists" within the Russian Empire?
column2: Muslim reformers who wanted modernized Islam to lead their societies.

column1: Term: Real wage
column2: Definition: A measure of wages that reflects the actual quantity of goods they can purchase.

column1: What 1905 event involved a procession of workers being attacked by police at the Winter Palace, leading to over 100 deaths?
column2: Bloody Sunday

column1: What were the three primary demands of the St Petersburg workers during the 1905 strike?
column2: A reduction of the working day to eight hours, an increase in wages, and improved working conditions.

column1: What elected consultative parliament did the Tsar allow to be created following the 1905 Revolution?
column2: The Duma

column1: How did the Tsar ensure the third Duma was less likely to challenge his authority?
column2: He changed the voting laws and packed it with conservative politicians, keeping out liberals and revolutionaries.

column1: Which three countries formed the Central Powers during the First World War?
column2: Germany, Austria, and Turkey

column1: Why was the city of St Petersburg renamed to Petrograd during the First World War?
column2: To remove the German association of the original name due to high anti-German sentiment.

column1: What was the impact of the Russian army's "scorched earth" retreat during WWI on the civilian population?
column2: The destruction of crops and buildings led to over 3 million refugees within Russia.

column1: Why did industrial equipment disintegrate more rapidly in Russia than elsewhere during the First World War?
column2: Russia was cut off from suppliers of industrial goods by German control of the Baltic Sea.

column1: What was the immediate cause of the bread riots in Russian cities by the winter of 1916?
column2: Large supplies of grain were diverted to feed the army, making bread and flour scarce for urban citizens.

column1: Which group of people led many of the strikes in Petrograd on 23 February 1917, now celebrated as International Women's Day?
column2: Women factory workers

column1: What happened to the Russian monarchy on 2 March 1917?
column2: Tsar Nicholas II abdicated his throne following the advice of military commanders.

column1: What were the three specific demands known as Lenin's "April Theses"?
column2: Bringing the war to a close, transferring land to the peasants, and nationalizing the banks.

column1: Why did Lenin propose renaming the Bolshevik Party to the Communist Party in April 1917?
column2: To indicate the party's new radical aims and distinguish it from other socialist groups.

column1: What were the "July Days" in 1917 Petrograd?
column2: A period of popular demonstrations staged by Bolsheviks that were sternly repressed by the Provisional Government.

column1: Who led the Military Revolutionary Committee appointed by the Petrograd Soviet to organize the October Revolution?
column2: Leon Trotskii

column1: What was the outcome of the Bolshevik uprising in Petrograd on 24 October 1917?
column2: The Bolsheviks seized government offices, arrested ministers, and took control of the city by nightfall.

column1: How did the Russian calendar system change in February 1918?
column2: The country switched from the Julian calendar to the Gregorian calendar, which was 13 days ahead.

column1: What major economic change did the Bolsheviks implement in November 1917 regarding the financial sector?
column2: They nationalized most banks, placing them under government ownership and management.

column1: Term: Budeonovka
column2: Definition: The specific Soviet hat chosen for the new army and official uniforms in 1918.

column1: Why did Lenin dismiss the Constituent Assembly in January 1918?
column2: The Assembly rejected Bolshevik measures, and Lenin believed the Congress of Soviets was a more democratic representative body.

column1: Which group in the Russian Civil War was known as the "Greens"?
column2: The Socialist Revolutionaries

column1: Who were the "Whites" in the context of the Russian Civil War?
column2: The pro-Tsarist forces who supported the autocracy and opposed the Bolsheviks.

column1: Which foreign nations sent troops to support the "Whites" during the Russian Civil War out of fear of socialism?
column2: France, USA, Britain, and Japan

column1: In the context of the Soviet Union, what does the term "autonomy" refer to for non-Russian nationalities?
column2: The right given to these nationalities to govern themselves within the Soviet state.

column1: What Bolshevik policy regarding traditional lifestyles was particularly unpopular in Central Asia?
column2: The harsh discouragement of nomadism.

column1: When was the Soviet Union (USSR) officially created from the former Russian Empire?
column2: December 1922

column1: What was the role of the "Cheka" in the early Bolshevik state?
column2: It was the secret police used to punish those who criticized the Bolsheviks.

column1: According to source material, why were many Bolshevik supporters in the army deserting during the Civil War?
column2: They were mostly peasants who wished to go home for the promised land redistribution.

column1: How did the Bolsheviks change the ownership of large houses in cities after the October Revolution?
column2: They enforced the partition of large houses according to family size and requirements.

column1: What was the main reason the "Whites" lost popular support during the Russian Civil War?
column2: They took harsh steps against peasants who had seized land, which alienated the rural population.
